name: Sinus Bradycardia
identifyBy: Upright P waves in II precede every QRS - normal morphology
Clinically significant if symptomatic: dizziness, syncope, hypotension, chest pain
management: Symptomatic (hypotension, syncope, altered consciousness): Atropine 0.6–1.2mg IV
Identify and treat reversible causes: drugs (beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, digoxin), hypothyroidism, hypothermia, raised ICP
Inferior STEMI: bradycardia due to vagal tone - Atropine usually effective
pearls: Sinus bradycardia in athletes at rest is physiological (high vagal tone) - HR may be 35–45 bpm
Chronotropic incompetence: failure to accelerate HR appropriately with exercise - sinus node disease
Inferior MI bradycardia: Bezold-Jarisch reflex - treat with atropine and IV fluid
Hypothermia: bradycardia expected at core temp <35°C - Osborn waves may co-exist
Post-cardiac arrest: sinus bradycardia common - ensure adequate perfusion pressure
differentials: 2° or 3° AV block (P waves present but not all conducting)
Junctional rhythm (retrograde or absent P waves)

qaNotes: Sinus rhythm family: P before every QRS unless sinus-node dysfunction pause pattern is specified.
